summary: The island capital of the Aztec Empire, built in Lake Texcoco after its founders saw an eagle eating a snake atop a cactus -- the vision that gives Mexico its modern flag. Clues most often identify it through that founding myth, its chinampa "floating gardens," its division into campan/calpulli districts, the Templo Mayor at its Sacred Precinct, its rulers Itzcoatl, Moctezuma II, and Cuauhtemoc, and its fall to Hernan Cortes in 1521, after which it became the site of modern Mexico City.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Clues most commonly identify the city as an artificial-island settlement in Lake Texcoco and the capital of the Aztec Empire, ruled by tlatoque (rulers) including Cuauhtemoc and Moctezuma. Giveaway lines regularly note that the city became Mexico City after the Spanish conquest, while mid-round clues cite a comparison of the city to Seville or Cordova and note that the disputed etymology of its name is debated via the "Bancroft dialogues."

clues.clue: Built on an artificial island (or islands) in Lake Texcoco, the capital of the Aztec Empire
examples: name this city built on an artificial island in Lake Texcoco, the capital of the Aztec empire.
name this city built on artificial islands called chinampas, the capital of the Aztec empire.

clues.clue: Became Mexico City after the Spanish conquest
examples: name this Mesoamerican city that became Mexico City.

clues.clue: Ruled by tlatoque (rulers) including Cuauhtemoc and Moctezuma
examples: name this city ruled by Cuauhtemoc and tlatoani like Moctezuma.

clues.clue: Described in one source as "as large as Seville or Cordova," with further information found in the Codex Mendoza
examples: In one source, this city was described as 'as large as Seville or Cordova', and more information about this city is found in the Codex Mendoza.

clues.clue: The "Bancroft dialogues" challenge the prevailing theory of the etymology of the city's name
examples: The 'Bancroft dialogues' challenge the prevailing theory of the etymology of this city's name.

works.name: Founding Myth
works.description: Tenochtitlan's founding legend holds that its migrating founders settled where they saw an eagle eating a snake while perched on a cactus growing from a rock in Lake Texcoco -- an image later adopted as Mexico's national emblem. Clues also note a darker origin story: the settlers had recently been driven out of Culhuacan after sacrificing the local leader's daughter and dressing a priest in her flayed skin.

clues.clue: Founders settled where they saw an eagle eating a snake while perched atop a cactus, on an island in Lake Texcoco
examples: This capital of the Triple Alliance was built on an island in Lake Texcoco where settlers allegedly saw an eagle eating a snake atop a cactus.
name this city founded where a snake-eating eagle was perched on a cactus, the Aztec capital.

clues.clue: The city's founders had been exiled from Culhuacan after sacrificing the local leader's daughter and wearing her flayed skin
examples: This city was settled shortly after its founders were exiled for sacrificing the daughter of the leader of Culhuacan and wearing her flayed skin.

works.name: Urban Layout and Infrastructure
works.description: The city was administratively divided into four zones (called campans or camps), each further split into about twenty calpulli districts, together housing an estimated 200,000+ residents connected by removable wooden bridges. Its chinampa "floating gardens," created by fencing off sections of lakebed, supplied agriculture and are still visible today in neighboring Lake Xochimilco. Fresh water arrived via a dual-pipe clay (later stone) aqueduct fed by springs at Chapultepec ("grasshopper hill"), while a ten-mile dike associated with the allied ruler Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco separated the lake's saltwater and freshwater sections. The city also ran an elaborate sanitation system and educated its youth at separate "Houses."

clues.clue: Divided into four zones (campans/camps), further split into a total of roughly 80 calpulli districts (about 20 each), housing over 200,000 people and linked by removable wooden bridges
examples: This city was divided into 4 sections called campans, and each campan was further split into 20 calpulli.
This city was divided into four zones called camps and eighty districts accessible by removable wooden bridges.

clues.clue: Agriculture relied on chinampa "floating gardens," created by fencing off sections of the lakebed
examples: There were an awful lot of chinampas in this Aztec capital city located on Lake Texcoco, where they were created by fencing in sections of the lake bed.
Chinampas were central to the agriculture of this city, the capital of the Aztec empire.

clues.clue: Supplied by a dual-pipe aqueduct (originally clay, later rebuilt in stone after a flood) that carried spring water from Chapultepec ("grasshopper hill")
examples: This city was supplied with water by a dual-pipe aqueduct made of clay that was rebuilt in stone after it was washed away by a flood.
During an attack on this city, one general destroyed an aqueduct designed with two ducts that provided spring water from 'grasshopper hill.'

clues.clue: A ten-mile dike, associated with the allied ruler Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco, separated the lake's saltwater and freshwater sections
examples: The residents of this city built a 10-mile-long dike to separate a neighboring saltwater lake from its namesake freshwater lake.
This city constructed a levee named for an allied ruler that split the freshwater and saltwater sections of a nearby lake.

clues.clue: A daily crew of about 1,000 people swept the streets and collected biodegradable trash, which was incinerated for lighting
examples: A thousand-person crew swept the streets of this city daily and collected biodegradable trash to be incinerated for lighting.

clues.clue: Canoes collected human urine (used as a fabric-dye mordant) and excrement (used as fertilizer) in a reportedly zero-waste system
examples: This city's thorough waste management system used canoes to collect human urine to use as a mordant in fabric dying as well as excrement for fertilizer, creating zero toxic waste.

clues.clue: Chinampa structures on which the city was built are still visible in neighboring Lake Xochimilco
examples: Lake Xochimilco still contains examples of the structures upon which this city was built in neighboring Lake Texcoco.

clues.clue: Boys and girls were educated separately, at "Houses of Youth" and "Houses of Song" respectively
examples: This city's boys and girls were educated at 'Houses of Youth' and 'Houses of Song', respectively.

works.name: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct
works.description: At the city's center stood the walled Sacred Precinct, home to the Templo Mayor pyramid, a dwelling for Eagle Warriors, and the Hueyi Tzompantli ("Great Skull Rack"), estimated to have held some 60,000 skulls from sacrificial victims. The pyramid's twin shrines were dedicated to the rain god Tlaloc and the war god Huitzilopochtli (the "left-handed hummingbird"). Excavation of the precinct resumed in earnest in 1978 after utility workers uncovered the Coyolxauhqui Stone, an 8.5-ton disc depicting the dismembered moon goddess Coyolxauhqui, who according to myth led her 400 brothers in an attack on their mother before being defeated by Huitzilopochtli.

clues.clue: The Sacred Precinct held a dwelling for the Eagle Warriors and the Hueyi Tzompantli ("Great Skull Rack"), estimated to hold about 60,000 sacrificial skulls
examples: The Sacred Precinct of a temple in this city had a dwelling for Eagle Warriors as well as a huge skull rack, and was built on the site where an eagle had been sighted on a cactus holding a snake.
The Hueyi Tzompantli, or 'Great Skull Rack', of this city was estimated to contain 60,000 skulls from sacrificial victims, many of whom were probably killed on the Templo Mayor.

clues.clue: The Templo Mayor bore twin shrines to the rain god Tlaloc and the war god Huitzilopochtli, the "left-handed hummingbird"
examples: Half of the Templo Mayor in this city was dedicated to a 'left-handed hummingbird' god.
Shrines to Tlaloc and Huitzilopochtli were built on top of the Templo Mayor, the largest pyramid in this Aztec capital.

clues.clue: Excavation resumed in 1978 after utility workers discovered the Coyolxauhqui Stone, an 8.5-ton disc depicting the dismembered moon goddess who led her 400 brothers to decapitate their mother
examples: An excavation of this city was restarted in 1978 after a stone disc portraying the death of a moon goddess was discovered by utility workers.
A stone at the base of a temple in this city depicts the dismemberment of a moon goddess who led her 400 brothers to decapitate their mother.

clues.clue: An artifact possibly representing the southern stars via a goddess's marigold headdress was found in a Sacred Precinct structure emulating the mythological "Serpent Mountain"
examples: An artifact from this city may have represented the southern stars through a goddess's headdress of marigolds, and was found in a structure in this city that emulated a mythological 'Serpent Mountain.'

clues.clue: A blue water pot found in the city was dedicated to the rain god Tlaloc, whose first wife was kidnapped by a night god before he married a jade-skirted goddess
examples: A blue water pot found in this city was dedicated to a rain god who, after his first wife was kidnapped by a night god, married a jade-skirted goddess.

works.name: The Triple Alliance and Rise to Power
works.description: Tenochtitlan's rise began under the tlatoani Itzcoatl, who organized the Triple Alliance with the cities of Texcoco (led by Nezahualcoyotl) and Tlacopan to defeat the dominant Tepanec city of Azcapotzalco. After that victory, Tenochtitlan emerged as the most powerful city in the Valley of Mexico and the seat of what became the Aztec Empire.

clues.clue: Itzcoatl organized the Triple Alliance (with Texcoco and Tlacopan) against the Tepanec city of Azcapotzalco
examples: Name this city whose king Itzcoatl organized a triple alliance of cities to combat the influence of the Tepanec empire. It was the site of the great Templo Mayor dedicated to Huitzilopochtli.

clues.clue: Itzcoatl's home city; after the Triple Alliance defeated Azcapotzalco, this city became the most powerful in the Valley of Mexico and the future site of Mexico City
examples: Itzcoatl was from this city. After the Triple Alliance's defeat of Azcapotzalco, this city on an island in Lake Texcoco became the most powerful in the region and future site of Mexico City.

works.name: Rulers of Tenochtitlan
works.description: Among the city's tlatoque, one ruler (Ahuizotl) took his name from a mythical man-eating dog said to live underwater and added the House of the Eagle Warriors to his rebuilding of the Templo Mayor. The best-known ruler, Moctezuma II, kept two palace zoos -- one for birds of prey, one for other animals -- that together employed over 300 people. After the conquest, indigenous "judge-governors" continued to rule the city on Spain's behalf: one, remembered by the nickname "mushroom" after losing a court case over a head tax, was badly injured falling off a roof during a mock sword fight and later commissioned a featherwork Mass of St. Gregory as a gift for Pope Paul III; a successor presided over a devastating hemorrhagic "great pestilence," identified by a 2018 study as Salmonella. The last independent tlatoani, Cuauhtemoc, was eventually hanged from a ceiba tree in Acalan, and his treasure was later seized by the pirate Jean Fleury.

clues.clue: One ruler took his name from a mythical man-eating dog said to live underwater
examples: One ruler of this city took his name from a mythical man-eating dog that lives underwater.

clues.clue: Ahuizotl added the House of the Eagle Warriors to his rebuilding of the Templo Mayor
examples: Ahuizotl added the House of the Eagle Warriors to his version of the Templo Mayor in this capital city of the Aztecs, built on an island in Lake Texcoco.

clues.clue: The best-known ruler (Moctezuma II) kept two palace zoos -- one for birds of prey, one for other animals -- that together employed over 300 people
examples: The most famous ruler of this city had two zoos in his palace, one for birds of prey and the other for all other animals, that together employed over 300 people.

clues.clue: A post-conquest indigenous "judge-governor," nicknamed "mushroom" after losing a court case over a head tax, was badly injured falling off a roof during a mock sword fight, and later commissioned a featherwork Mass of St. Gregory as a gift for Pope Paul III
examples: A ruler of this city was badly injured after falling off a roof during a sword fight with imaginary opponents and got the nickname 'mushroom' for losing a court case against an onerous head tax.
As a gift for Paul III, a ruler of this city commissioned a featherwork rendition of The Mass of St. Gregory.

clues.clue: The second post-conquest "judge-governor" presided over a hemorrhagic "great pestilence" identified by a 2018 study as Salmonella
examples: The second 'judge-governor' of this city presided over a hemorrhagic 'great pestilence' that a 2018 study identified as Salmonella.

clues.clue: The last independent tlatoani, Cuauhtemoc, was eventually hanged from a ceiba tree in Acalan, and his treasure was seized by the pirate Jean Fleury
examples: Jean Fleury captured the treasure of a ruler of this city who was hanged from a ceiba tree in Acalan.

works.name: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the City
works.description: Hernan Cortes's forces, aided by Pedro de Alvarado, massacred noblemen and warriors at the Great Temple during a religious festival while Moctezuma II was held hostage; foreign accounts claim Moctezuma was later stoned to death by his own subjects, prompting the Spanish to flee the city via its western causeway on La Noche Triste. The city ultimately fell after a 75-day siege by Cortes. The conquistador Bernal Diaz del Castillo, who claimed to have fought in over 130 battles in Mexico, later chronicled the campaign, including the final siege, in his True History of the Conquest of New Spain.

clues.clue: Pedro de Alvarado's troops massacred noblemen and warriors at the Great Temple during a religious festival
examples: Name this city where many noblemen and warriors were massacred by the troops of Pedro de Alvarado during a religious festival in its 'Great Temple.'

clues.clue: Foreign accounts claim a ruler was stoned to death by his own subjects, and the occupying Spanish army fled the city via its western causeway on La Noche Triste
examples: Foreign accounts claim that a ruler of this city was stoned to death by his subjects, forcing an occupying army to flee this city via its western causeway on La Noche Triste.

clues.clue: The city fell after a 75-day siege by Hernan Cortes
examples: Ahuizotl added the House of the Eagle Warriors to his version of the Templo Mayor in this capital city of the Aztecs, built on an island in Lake Texcoco, which was put under a 75-day siege by Hernan Cortes.

clues.clue: The conquistador Bernal Diaz del Castillo, who claimed to have fought in over 130 battles in Mexico, chronicled the final siege of the city in his True History of the Conquest of New Spain
examples: Diaz was allegedly present at over 130 battles in Mexico, including the final siege of this Aztec capital, which was followed by La Noche Triste after Alvarado was left in charge.

works.name: Legacy
works.description: Aztec goods taken from the city reached Europe during the conquest era: a royal tour brought them to cities including Brussels, where they "rejoiced the heart" of the German artist Albrecht Durer.

clues.clue: Goods from the city "rejoiced [the] heart" of Albrecht Durer when a royal tour brought them to cities including Brussels
examples: Goods from this city 'rejoiced [the] heart' of Albrecht Durer when a royal tour brought them to cities like Brussels.

comprehensive_summary: Tenochtitlan was the island capital of the Aztec Empire, founded on an island in Lake Texcoco after its migrating settlers saw an eagle eating a snake atop a cactus -- an image later adopted as Mexico's national emblem -- and only shortly after the same group had been exiled from Culhuacan for sacrificing the local leader's daughter and wearing her flayed skin. The city was organized into four zones (campans), each divided into about twenty calpulli districts, together housing over 200,000 residents linked by removable wooden bridges; its chinampa "floating gardens," created by fencing off sections of lakebed, supplied its agriculture and remain visible today in neighboring Lake Xochimilco. Fresh water arrived via a dual-pipe clay (later stone) aqueduct fed by springs at Chapultepec, while a ten-mile dike associated with the allied Texcocan ruler Nezahualcoyotl separated the lake's saltwater and freshwater sections; the city also ran an elaborate sanitation system, with a daily 1,000-person street-sweeping crew and canoes that collected urine and excrement for reuse, and educated its youth separately at "Houses of Youth" and "Houses of Song." At the city's heart stood the Sacred Precinct, dominated by the Templo Mayor pyramid with twin shrines to the rain god Tlaloc and the war god Huitzilopochtli, a dwelling for the Eagle Warriors, and the Hueyi Tzompantli skull rack, estimated to hold 60,000 sacrificial skulls; major excavation resumed there in 1978 after utility workers uncovered the 8.5-ton Coyolxauhqui Stone depicting the dismembered moon goddess. The city's rise to regional dominance began when the tlatoani Itzcoatl organized the Triple Alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan to defeat the Tepanec city of Azcapotzalco, after which Tenochtitlan became the Valley of Mexico's most powerful city. Later rulers included Ahuizotl, who took his name from a legendary man-eating water monster and added the House of the Eagle Warriors to the Templo Mayor, and the best-known tlatoani Moctezuma II, who kept two palace zoos employing over 300 people. During the Spanish conquest, Pedro de Alvarado's troops massacred noblemen and warriors during a religious festival at the Great Temple while Hernan Cortes held Moctezuma II hostage; foreign accounts claim Moctezuma was later stoned to death by his own subjects, prompting the Spanish to retreat via the city's western causeway on La Noche Triste. The city ultimately fell after a 75-day siege by Cortes, an episode chronicled by the conquistador Bernal Diaz del Castillo in his True History of the Conquest of New Spain; its last independent tlatoani, Cuauhtemoc, was eventually hanged from a ceiba tree in Acalan, and his treasure was later seized by the pirate Jean Fleury. After the conquest, the city continued under indigenous "judge-governors": one, nicknamed "mushroom" after losing a court case over a head tax, commissioned a featherwork Mass of St. Gregory as a gift for Pope Paul III, and a successor presided over a devastating epidemic later identified as Salmonella. Aztec goods from the city that reached Europe during the conquest era reportedly "rejoiced the heart" of Albrecht Durer when displayed in Brussels, and the city itself became the site of modern Mexico City.
recursive_suggestions: Zocalo of Mexico City -- surfaced as its own dense answerline (Templo Mayor excavation, cathedral, Boy Heroes/Plaza of Three Cultures-adjacent content); excluded here as a distinct, mostly-modern Geography/place answerline rather than folded into pre-conquest Tenochtitlan.
Mexico City -- surfaced as a separate, largely modern-era answerline (1968 Olympics, 1985 earthquake, Tlatelolco 1968 massacre, 1967 Treaty of Tlatelolco); excluded here, would support its own topic.

Aztec emperors / tlatoani -- the title itself, and a biographical cluster of emperors (Moctezuma I, Moctezuma II, Ahuizotl, Cuauhtemoc collectively), surfaced as independent answerlines; only facts where the answerline was literally Tenochtitlan were kept here.

Ahuizotl and Moctezuma I -- each has independent bonus-part answerline presence (temple of skulls, Flowery War with Tlaxcala, Huastec conquest) not folded in here since their answerline was the ruler, not the city.

Huitzilopochtli / Aztec mythology -- Coyolxauhqui, Huitzilopochtli's birth and war with his 400 brothers, and Tlaloc's myth cycle surfaced independently as Mythology-category answerlines; only city-specific facts (shrine locations, artifact finds) were kept here.

Ambiguity flag: 'San Lorenzo Tenochtitlan,' the first Olmec capital, shares the word 'Tenochtitlan' but is an entirely unrelated site/civilization (per one bonus's own text, 'not to be confused with the area of the same name that now forms the centre of Ciudad de Mexico'); excluded as a false-positive name match, not folded in.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo -- has independent literary/historical presence as chronicler of the conquest; could support a standalone figure page.
Texcoco and Tlacopan -- the other two Triple Alliance cities, and Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco specifically, surfaced with independent clue content not pursued here.
Tarascan (Purepecha) Empire -- Aztec rival based in Michoacan, surfaced independently in a bonus about Ahuizotl; noted but not developed here.
